1: ʿaṣmᵉḵā ûḇāśārᵉḵā (‘your bone and your flesh’) is the OT’s covenant-kinship formula (Gen 2:23 Adam of Eve; Judg 9:2; 2 Sam 5:1). The elders appeal to shared descent to legitimate David’s kingship over all Israel after Saul’s collapse — the same formula the parallel passage (2 Sam 5:1) uses. Chronicles omits all of David’s Hebron years and Ishbosheth’s rival reign, moving immediately to this moment of national unity.
2: ʾattāh hûʾ hammôṣîʾ wᵉhammēḇîʾ (‘you were the one who led out and brought in Israel’) — military idiom for commanding an army (Num 27:17; 1 Sam 18:13). The elders recall David’s past military leadership under Saul as grounds for accepting him as king — he was the real commander all along.
3: wayyikrōt dāwid lāhem bᵉrît biḥeḇrôn lipnê YHWH — David cuts a covenant with the elders ‘before the LORD’ at Hebron. The Davidic kingship is established as a covenant act witnessed by YHWH, not merely a political agreement. kᵉḏiḇar YHWH bᵉyaḏ šᵉmûʾēl — ‘according to the word of YHWH by the hand of Samuel’ — Chronicles frames the event as prophetic fulfillment from the start.
4: yᵉrûšālaim hiʾ yᵉḇûs — ‘Jerusalem, that is Jebus’ — Chronicles identifies Jerusalem by its pre-conquest name (Judges 19:10-11). The Jebusites had held the city since the conquest. The Chronicler begins David’s reign with the capture of YHWH’s eventual throne city.
5: lōʾ tāḇôʾ hēnnāh — ‘you shall not come in here’ — the Jebusites’ taunt; in 2 Sam 5:6 they add ‘even the blind and the lame will ward you off’. Chronicles omits the taunt content and focuses on the outcome: wayyilkaḏ dāwid ʾet maṣṣaḏat ṣiyyôn — ‘David captured the stronghold of Zion’ — establishing the theological center of the Davidic kingdom.
6: The appointment formula: kol makkēh yᵉḇûsî bārîʾšônāh yihyeh lᵉrōʾš wᵉlᵉśār — ‘whoever strikes the Jebusites first shall become head and commander.’ This is a meritocratic military appointment. Joab (ben-ṣᵉrûyāh — always identified by his mother, suggesting known status) wins the position. The parallel in 2 Sam 5:8 is obscure; Chronicles’ version is clearer about the merit-based appointment.
7: miṣṣaḏāh dāwid — ‘from the stronghold, David [dwelt]’ — the fortress that had been Jebus becomes the ‘City of David.’ The renaming establishes the theological and dynastic identity of Jerusalem. The Chronicler uses this transition as the foundation of the temple-building narrative that dominates chs. 22–29.
8: Joab is credited with repairing sheʾār hāʿîr (‘the rest of the city’) while David builds from the Millo inward. hammillōʾ is likely a terraced fill structure supporting the city expansion (cf. 2 Sam 5:9). The collaboration of king and general in urban construction shows the Chronicler’s interest in organized communal building — anticipating the temple project.
9: wayyēlek dāwid hālôḵ wᵉḡāḏôl wᵉYHWH ṣᵉḇāʾôt ʿimmô — ‘David kept going and growing, and the LORD of hosts was with him.’ The divine-warrior formula (YHWH ṣᵉḇāʾôt = LORD of armies) grounds David’s expansion not in military genius but in divine presence. This is the Chronicler’s theological explanation for all Davidic success.
10: rāʾšê haggibborîm — ‘chiefs of the mighty men’ — introduces the famous warrior roster. ʾăšer ḥizzᵉqû-ʿimmô ḇamlûḵātô — ‘who strengthened themselves with him in his kingship’ — the Chronicler frames the Mighty Men as co-participants in establishing the Davidic kingdom, consistent with his overall emphasis on unified all-Israel support for David.
11: jashobeam ben-ḥakmonî — head of the three (or thirty, depending on text). The phrase rōʾš haššālišîm is disputed: ‘chief of the thirty’ (MT šᵉlōšîm) vs ‘chief of the three’ (LXX and some MSS šᵉlōšāh); the parallel in 2 Sam 23:8 has ‘Josheb-basshebeth’ a different name for possibly the same person. His feat: slaying 300 with a single spear-thrust. The number is symbolic of overwhelming martial prowess.
12: ʾeleʿāzār ben-dôdô haʾăḥōḥî — Eleazar son of Dodo the Ahohite, ‘one of the three mighty men.’ His story follows in v. 13-14. The Ahohites were a Benjaminite sub-clan (8:4). These three men are haggibborîm at the highest tier — an elite within the elite.
13: The battle at Pas-dammim (here; 2 Sam 23:11 has Lehi) where the Israelites fled from the Philistines over a barley field. The geographical difference between the parallel accounts may reflect different battle sites or text corruption. wayyāqōmû bᵉtôḵ haḥelqāh wayyaṣṣîlûhā — ‘they took their stand in the middle of the field and saved it’ — collective action; v. 14 shows it was Eleazar specifically.
14: wayyaṣṣîlem YHWH tᵉšûʿāh gᵉḏôlāh — ‘and YHWH saved [them] with a great salvation.’ The military victory is attributed entirely to YHWH. tᵉšûʿāh (salvation/deliverance) is the same word-root as yēšûaʿ (Joshua/Jesus). This divine-salvation formula in battle contexts establishes the theological register of all Davidic warfare.
15: Three of the Thirty go to David at the cave of Adullam while the Philistines are encamped in the valley of Rephaim. The rock (haṣṣûr) is identified with Adullam. The detail that maṣṣaḇ pᵉlištîm bᵉḇêt leḥem — ‘the Philistine garrison was at Bethlehem’ — marks how completely the enemy had occupied David’s home territory, heightening the poignancy of his longing.
16: David is ‘in the stronghold’ (bammᵉṣûḏāh) while Philistine neṣîḇ (garrison/prefect) holds Bethlehem. The contrast between the cave-king and the occupied homeland generates the narrative tension that leads to David’s thirst and its extraordinary fulfillment.
17: mî yašqēnî mayim mibôr bêt-leḥem — ‘Who will give me water to drink from the well of Bethlehem at the gate?’ This is an offhand expression of longing, not a command — which makes the three warriors’ response more extraordinary. The well at the gate was a civic landmark; the gate = the place of legal assembly and public life.
18: The three break through the Philistine garrison, draw water from Bethlehem’s well, and bring it to David. David’s response: wayyissᵉḵēhû lAYHWH — ‘he poured it out to YHWH.’ This is a libation — treating the water as too holy to drink because it was obtained at the risk of blood. The act transforms water into a sacred offering, converting an act of loyalty into an act of worship.
19: ḥālîlāh lî mēʾĕlōhay — ‘God forbid it of me / my God, may I not do this.’ ḥālîlāh is a strong profanation-avowal formula (Gen 44:7; Josh 22:29; 1 Sam 12:23). hᵉdām hāʾănāšîm hāʾēlleh bᵉnapšôtām — ‘the blood of these men at the cost of their lives (napšôtām).’ David refuses to consume what was purchased at the risk of life — a profound meditation on value, sacrifice, and the sacred cost of devotion.
20: ʾăḇîšay ʾăḥî yôʾāḇ — Abishai brother of Joab. He ‘lifted up his spear against three hundred and killed them.’ He is among haššᵉlōšāh (the three) but his status is complex: v. 21 says he was ‘more honored than the two’ (than Eleazar and Jashobeam? or the other two of the second set of three?). The text is difficult; the Chronicler seems to distinguish two tiers of three.
21: min-haššᵉlōšāh nikkāḇāḏ — ‘more honored than the three’ — but Abishai ‘did not attain to the first three’ (wᵉʾel-haššᵉlōšāh lōʾ-bāʾ). He was śar (officer/captain) of the three. The hierarchical ranking within the Mighty Men reflects a formal military structure.
22: bᵉnāyāhû ben-yᵉhôyāḏāʿ ben-ʾîš-ḥayil — ‘Benaiah son of Jehoiada, son of a valiant man.’ His three exploits: (1) slaying two ʾărîʾēl of Moab (lion-like men or ‘altar-hearths’ of Moab — meaning debated); (2) slaying a lion in a pit on a snowy day (bᵉyôm haššāleg); (3) slaying a large Egyptian (v. 23). These are listed in ascending difficulty.
23: Benaiah kills an Egyptian ‘a man of measure’ (ʾîš middāh — very tall, same formula as Goliath, 1 Chr 20:6) with the Egyptian’s own spear. wayyigzōl ʾet haḥănît miyyaḏ hammiṣrî wayyahaṣṣᵉhû baḥănîtô — ‘he wrested the spear from the Egyptian’s hand and killed him with his own spear.’ The reversal motif (turning an enemy’s weapon against him) appears also in David vs. Goliath and has symbolic resonance: the victor uses the enemy’s own power for his destruction. [Existing entry preserved]
24: ʾēlleh ʿāśāh bᵉnāyāhû — ‘these things Benaiah did’ — formal summary of v. 22-23. wayyᵉhî-lô šēm bᵉšᵉlōšāh haggibborîm — ‘he had a name among the thirty mighty men’ (MT ‘three’ — text uncertain as at v. 11). His fame is specifically named, not just assumed.
25: Benaiah ‘was honored beyond the thirty’ (nikkāḇāḏ hûʾ min-haššᵉlōšîm) but did not attain to the Three. David set him over his bodyguard (mišmaʿtô). This administrative note shows the Chronicler’s interest in the organization of David’s court — which directly relates to his preparations for temple governance.
26: The roster of ‘valiant men of the armies’ (gibbôrê haḥăyālîm) begins. These are the Thirty (roughly) — a second tier below the Three. ʾăśāhʾēl ʾăḥî yôʾāḇ — Asahel brother of Joab, who famously died in 2 Sam 2 pursuing Abner. His inclusion here, alongside Elhanan of Bethlehem, begins a list that will end with Uriah the Hittite (v. 41).
27: Shammoth the Harorite and Helez the Pelonite — geographical epithets identify their tribal origins. The parallel in 2 Sam 23:25-26 has slightly variant names (Shammah the Harodite / Helez the Paltite). The differences likely reflect copyist variants in proper names, a common phenomenon in these lists.
28: Ira son of Ikkesh the Tekoite and Abiezer the Anathothite. Anathoth is the priestly town later famous as Jeremiah’s birthplace (Jer 1:1). The warrior list crosses geographic lines — Tekoa, Anathoth, and many other towns — showing David’s coalition of all-Israel support.
29: Sibbecai the Hushathite — who will appear in ch. 20:4 killing Sippai the giant. Ilai the Ahohite. The Hushathites are a Judahite sub-group (1 Chr 4:4). The recurrence of Ahohite warriors (cf. v. 12) suggests a clan with a strong martial tradition.
30: Maharai the Netophathite and Heled son of Baanah the Netophathite. Netophah is near Bethlehem (Ezra 2:22); the two Netophathites together suggest geographic clustering within the list.
31: Ithai son of Ribai of Gibeah — Gibeah is Saul’s hometown (1 Sam 10:26), so this Benjaminite warrior serves David even from the house of Saul’s city. Benaiah the Pirathonite (Ephraim, Judg 12:15). The list’s geographic breadth — from Gibeah to Pirathon — reflects all-Israel participation.
32: Hurai of the brooks of Gaash and Abiel the Arbathite. ‘Brooks of Gaash’ references the area where Joshua was buried (Josh 24:30). The Arbathite is from Beth-arabah (Josh 15:6), near the Dead Sea rift.
33: Azmaveth the Baharumite (2 Sam 23:31 has ‘Barhumite’ — likely variant of the same place, Bahurim, 2 Sam 3:16) and Eliahba the Shaalbonite (from Shaalabbin, Dan’s territory, Josh 19:42). Both names appear only in the Mighty Men lists.
34: Jonathan son of Shage the Hararite and ‘the sons of Hashem the Gizonite’ — Gizon is otherwise unattested in the OT; possibly a scribal corruption. The Hararites appear several times in the list (vv. 34-35), suggesting a warrior clan from the hill country.
35: Ahiam son of Sacar the Hararite and Eliphal son of Ur. The 2 Sam 23:33 parallel has ‘Ahiam son of Sharar the Hararite’ — the variant between Sacar and Sharar is a typical copyist confusion of kāp̄ and rēš. These variants are numerous throughout the parallel lists and generally have no theological significance.
36: Hepher the Mecherathite (unattested elsewhere) and Ahijah the Pelonite. The Pelonite appears again at v. 27 (Helez). The frequent ‘-ite’ demonymic forms indicate these warriors are identified by their home towns or clans, not by patronymics — suggesting some are from small or obscure communities.
37: Hezro the Carmelite (from Carmel in Judah, 1 Sam 25:2, Nabal’s home) and Naarai son of Ezbai. The Carmelite connection links to Abigail’s homeland. These warriors are from the Judean heartland.
38: Joel brother of Nathan — if Nathan here is David’s prophet, this would link a warrior directly to a royal courtier. More likely a different Nathan. Mibhar son of Hagri (2 Sam 23:36 reads differently — possibly scribal). The Hagrites were a trans-Jordanian people (1 Chr 5:10); a Hagrite’s son among David’s elite shows the ethnically inclusive character of the warrior band.
39: Zelek the Ammonite — an Ammonite among David’s elite warriors. Naharai the Berothite, armor-bearer of Joab son of Zeruiah. The presence of an Ammonite (enemy nation) and a servant of Joab in the Thirty reflects the diverse and personally loyal character of David’s military family.
40: Ira the Ithrite and Gareb the Ithrite — two warriors from the same clan appearing together. The Ithrites were a Judahite sub-group (2 Sam 23:38).
41: ʾûriyyāh haḥittî — Uriah the Hittite appears at the very end of the elite warrior roster. In 2 Sam 23:39, he is the thirty-seventh and last named. This placement is almost certainly intentional in the literary ordering — Uriah the Hittite, the most loyal of all the Mighty Men, the one David had murdered. The Chronicler reproduces the list including Uriah; he omits the Bathsheba narrative (as he omits David’s failures generally) but includes Uriah’s name in the honor roll.
42: Adina son of Shiza the Reubenite, chief of the Reubenites, and thirty with him. The Reubenite contingent has its own sub-commander. This entry marks the transition to a broader military survey that extends beyond the original core group.
43: Hanan son of Maacah and Joshaphat the Mithnite. Maacah is a place-name (Aram-maacah, north of Dan) as well as a personal name. These names appear only here.
44: Uzzia the Ashterathite (from Ashtaroth in Bashan, trans-Jordan — Manassite territory), Shama and Jeiel sons of Hothan the Aroerite (Aroer, different from Gad’s Aroer — possibly the Judean Aroer of 1 Sam 30:28).
45: Jediael son of Shimri and Joha his brother the Tizite. ‘Tizite’ is unattested elsewhere; possibly a corrupt form of another place-name.
46: Eliel the Mahavite; sons of Elnaam: Jeribai and Joshaviah; Ithmah the Moabite. Another Moabite (cf. Zelek the Ammonite, v. 39) in David’s corps — showing the expanding ethnic scope of the Davidic coalition. The Mahavite clan is otherwise unknown.
47: Eliel, Obed, and Jaasiel the Mesobaite conclude the list. ‘Mesobaite’ is unattested; possibly a place-name. The list of Mighty Men (vv. 26-47) totals approximately thirty-seven names, though some are grouped. The Chronicler uses this list to show all-Israel participation in David’s kingdom from the start — consistent with his broader theological agenda.
